The  XRD  technique  was  used  to  characterize  the  structural  properties  of  PLGA  NPs,  FI-PLGA  NPs,  and
FI  extract. The  XRD  pattern  of  PLGA  NPs  is  depicted  in  Fig.  4(b),  in  which  no  distinct  peak  was  observed,
indicating the amorphous nature of the polymer. Furthermore, FI-PLGA NPs also showed no characteristic peak
due to the amorphous nature of the drug. A reduction in spectrum intensity was detected as compared to the FI
extract, possibly due to the entrapment of the extract in the PLGA matrix.
Drug release study
The FI extract’s release from PLGA NPs was assessed in PBS (pH = 7.4) (Fig. 5a). The pH 7.4 was selected to
evaluate the release profile of the nanoparticles in a relevant biological environment. This helps in predicting
the drug behavior in systemic circulation before reaching the tumor microenvironment. After 96 h, a maximum
release of 99.08% FI extract was achieved. An initial burst release of 28.8% FI extract from PLGA nanoparticles
occurred within the first 12 h, likely due to drug molecules present on or near the nanoparticle surface. This
surface-bound drug rapidly diffused into the surrounding medium upon adding nanoparticles. Following the
initial burst, a slower and more sustained release was obtained, attributed to the gradual diffusion of FI extract
encapsulated  within  the  nanoparticle  core.  Moreover,  44.6%,  63.2%,  86.7%,  and  99.08%  of  FI  extract  were
released by degradation of the polymer matrix during 24, 48, 72 and 96 h.
Quantitative cellular uptake of FI extract and FI-PLGA NPs
The uptake time of FI and FI-PLGANPs at different concentrations (200, 25) µg/ml is presented in Fig. 5 (b and
c). No significant changes were seen between the two distinct concentrations of FI and FI-PLGA NPs, except that
absorption values were higher at the higher concentration (200 µg/ml). FI-PLGA NPs were internalized faster
in BT-474 cells compared to free FI extract, as nanoparticles in the size range of 100 to 400 nm are more rapidly
taken up by cells42. The maximum absorption occurred at 2 h for FI-PLGA NPs and 5 h for the free FI extract,
highlighting the optimal drug uptake time for each formulation. Moreover, the increased absorption value of
FI-PLGA NPs compared to free extract within the cells is attributed to the sustained release of the encapsulated
drug.
Qualitative cellular uptake of FI extract and FI-PLGA NPs
A  confocal  microscope  was  used  to  optimize  cellular  internalization  by  utilizing  the  FI  extract’s  intrinsic
fluorescence. This  study  revealed  that  FI-PLGA  NPs  presented  significantly  higher  cellular  uptake  compared
to free FI extract after 2 h of incubation. The fluorescence intensity of FI-PLGA NPs was noticeably enhanced,
indicating improved internalization within BT-474 cells (Fig. 6). In contrast, free FC exhibited a lower uptake,
suggesting  limited  cellular  permeability.  These  findings  confirm  that  PLGA  NPs  encapsulation  enhances  the
bioavailability and intracellular delivery of FI extract.
